claim: Agent definitions vary widely; range from simple LLM + chat interface to complex systems with persistent memory, learning, knowledge bases, and long-term independence
actionable: When discussing agents, clarify the definition being used; avoid assuming one definition applies across vendors, roles (dev vs sales), or use cases

claim: Degrees of agentic behavior exist on a spectrum: simple prompt + knowledge base, LLM alone, LLM with tools, LLM in loop with planning, autonomous multi-week systems
actionable: Classify agent capabilities on a spectrum rather than binary; this clarifies marketing hype vs technical reality and sets realistic expectations

claim: Distinguishing agents from LLMs is blurry; modern LLMs do planning, consume external data, and use reasoning loops—traits historically attributed to agents
actionable: Focus on specific capabilities (planning, reasoning, tool use, feedback loops) rather than trying to apply a single 'agent' label

claim: Agent pricing models are conflated with human worker salaries; long-term pricing converges toward marginal cost (LLM API costs) rather than labor replacement value
actionable: Don't assume agent pricing remains at human salary levels; expect price compression as market competition increases and marginal costs dominate

claim: AI augments human productivity rather than replacing humans outright; two humans doing a job may become one human using AI productively, or headcount growth slows rather than headcount declining
actionable: When evaluating agent ROI, measure productivity gains and slower hiring needs rather than assuming 1:1 human replacement

claim: Most AI agent work involves non-deterministic reasoning and decision-making; humans retain decision intent; humans still 'push the button' to start agents
actionable: Design agents for augmentation, not autonomy; expect humans to define goals, evaluate outputs, and authorize actions

claim: From outside, an agent and a function are indistinguishable if both take inputs and return outputs; internal architecture differs but external behavior may be similar
actionable: Abstract agents as functions; focus on input/output contracts rather than internal implementation; let the system handle LLM vs traditional code

claim: Agent access to data is constrained by data silos and security policies; walled gardens (e.g., iPhone photos) block agent access even if technically possible
actionable: Assume agents cannot access private/proprietary data without explicit API access; design agents around publicly available or explicitly authorized data

claim: Web browsing agents are slow and unreliable; they require frequent retries and screenshots sent back to the LLM, creating latency and cost inefficiencies
actionable: Avoid web-based agents for latency-sensitive tasks; prefer APIs or direct database access; invest in browser automation when web access is unavoidable

claim: Multi-modal agents (handling images, clicks, text) unlock new capabilities; visual understanding of web interfaces, device farms, and mobile apps requires more than text-based loops
actionable: Invest in multimodal models for agents that interact with visual interfaces; text-only agents are limited for web browsing and UI automation

claim: Consumer websites increasingly deploy anti-agent CAPTCHAs to prevent automated access; agent-human distinction may blur as agents improve at passing CAPTCHA challenges
actionable: Expect websites to block agents; be prepared for escalating anti-agent measures; plan for agents to have human-like authentication (SSO, MFA) capabilities

claim: Success looks like agents accessing all tools a human has access to on the user's behalf; critical unsolved problems: security, authentication, access control, data retention, consumer site blocking
actionable: Build agents that can act on behalf of users within authenticated, permissioned boundaries; focus on solving authentication and authorization challenges
